summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the n8n server fetches arbitrary internal URLs via GET /rest/workflows/from-url with zero SSRF protections. The exploit started an internal HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:9999, authenticated as a standard owner user, obtained a project ID, and sent a from-url request targeting the internal server. The n8n server fetched from localhost and returned the internal server's response containing the proof string EVOHUNT-SSRF-PROOF.
expected_security_impact: The n8n server should fetch data from an internal HTTP server running on 127.0.0.1:9999 and return the internal server's response data to the authenticated caller, demonstrating SSRF. The proof string EVOHUNT-SSRF-PROOF should appear in the response body.
actual_observed_result: Running the exploit against a locally built n8n v2.2.0 instance produced HTTP 200 with response body {"data":{"name":"SSRF Proof","nodes":[{"type":"n8n-nodes-base.manualTrigger","name":"EVOHUNT-SSRF-PROOF",...}],"connections":{},"settings":{}}}. The internal server log confirmed it received GET /ssrf-proof from the n8n server process, proving the server-side request was made to 127.0.0.1:9999.
verification_method: The exploit starts a local HTTP server on port 9999 that returns a workflow JSON containing the unique proof string 'EVOHUNT-SSRF-PROOF'. It then calls GET /rest/workflows/from-url?url=http://127.0.0.1:9999/ssrf-proof&projectId=<id> with the authenticated session cookie. If the response body contains the proof string, SSRF is confirmed - the n8n server fetched from localhost and returned internal data. The proof string is unique and would never appear in normal n8n responses.
evidence_produced_by_run: The internal HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:9999 logged receiving GET /ssrf-proof from the n8n server process.
The from-url endpoint returned HTTP 200 with the workflow JSON containing 'EVOHUNT-SSRF-PROOF' in the nodes array.
The n8n server made an outbound HTTP request to 127.0.0.1:9999 (localhost) based on the attacker-supplied url parameter.
No SSRF protections (private IP blocking, protocol filtering, redirect limits, timeouts) were applied to the axios.get() call.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned ref, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
